ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for nasi goreng chicken; closest parent 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002700) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fried rice) is the closest parent category; no specific nasi goreng chicken entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for nasi goreng chicken or fried rice as a prepared dish at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken nasi goreng as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall or casual restaurant, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng Ayam (Chicken Fried Rice) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo / HPB Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists nasi goreng (chicken) as a standard hawker dish. Values below are drawn from the HPB nutrient composition data for nasi goreng ayam, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for nasi goreng ayam to confirm plausibility. Per-100 g values are derived from the composite dish as reported. Minor rounding applied for consistency.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice / white rice-based mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi goreng chicken as a named dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white rice-based fried rice dishes with mixed protein, which cluster around GI 50–60 in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr, 2021). The addition of oil, egg, and chicken moderates the GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 41.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and kecap manis
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving of chicken nasi goreng provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing 64–98% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high glycemic load per serving from refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 (low GI category) and a glycemic load of ~41 per standard serving, chicken nasi goreng can cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 4.9 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken preparation, request less oil, and limit consumption frequency to support heart-healthy dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Chicken (Malay: Nasi Goreng Ayam; Chinese: 鸡肉炒饭) is a popular Southeast Asian fried rice dish widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore. It is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a hot wok with diced or shredded chicken, garlic, shallots, egg, and a seasoning blend that typically includes soy sauce, kecap manis (sweet soy sauce), and chilli paste or fresh chilli. The dish is characterised by its smoky wok-hei aroma, savoury-sweet flavour profile, and slightly chewy rice texture. It is commonly garnished with sliced cucumber, tomato, fried shallots, and a fried egg on top. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 570 kcal, 25 g protein, 20 g fat, and 75 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal. The sodium content is notably high (~1470 mg per serving) due to soy-based condiments, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low GI category), with a high glycemic load per serving (~41), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is typically halal and dairy-free but contains soy and egg allergens. It is a staple of Malaysian and Singaporean hawker culture and is served at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi goreng chicken?
Is nasi goreng suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium in nasi goreng?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are drawn from HPB Singapore Foodo and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for nasi goreng ayam as a composite hawker dish. The match is adapted rather than exact because restaurant-specific formulations vary; the HPB/MyFCD composite dish is the closest approved-source anchor. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice dishes, as no direct GI measurement for nasi goreng chicken exists in the approved GI database.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source databases for this dish. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with the HPB source for this dish category — no industrially produced trans fats are expected in a standard hawker preparation. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries; no direct measurement for nasi goreng chicken exists. 6. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce and kecap manis; restaurant formulations may vary significantly. 7. Food group has been reclassified from input 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' as the dominant macronutrient and caloric contribution is from white rice; chicken is a secondary component. 8. Halal status is assumed based on typical Malaysian/Singaporean hawker preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Identified that food_group in input ('Meat and alternatives') was inconsistent with the rice-dominant composition; reclassified to 'Grains and staples'.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback for q4_glycemic_completeness. Corrected 'gi_category' from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, aligning with standard GI classifications. Verified aliases_by_language consistency — all entries in aliases_by_language confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases list. Verified all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Verified trans_fat_g 0.0 documented. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings. Confirmed GI > 0 and gi_category is valid string. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 confirmed passing.
